subject: Physics
q: A triangular plate is shown. A force F→=4i^-3j^ is applied at point P. The torque at point P with respect to point O and Q are:
question: A triangular plate is shown. A force F→=4i^-3j^ is applied at point P. The torque at point P with respect to point O and Q are:
solution:
F→=4i^-3j^

r→1=5i^+53j^ and r→2=-5i^+53j^

Torque about 'O'

τ→O=r→1×F→=-15-203k^

Torque about 'Q'

τ→Q=r→2×F→=15-203k^

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is making pure rolling on a horizontal surface with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The velocity of centre of mass of the sphere will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is making pure rolling on a horizontal surface with kinetic energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2240</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The velocity of centre of mass of the sphere will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Let velocity of centre of mass of sphere be v.

Total kinetic energy of solid sphere is KE=12mv2+12Iω2, where, I is moment of inertia of the solid sphere about the axis passing through it's centre of mass and ω=vR pure rolling condition.

Therefore,

 2240=122(v)2+1225(2)R2vR2

⇒2240=v2+25v2

⇒v=40 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q: A solid cylinder having radius $R$ and length $L$ is slipping on a rough horizontal plane. At time $t=0$ the cylinder has a translational velocity $v_0=49$ m/s, perpendicular to its axis and a rotational velocity $v_0/4R$ about the centre. The time taken by the cylinder to start rolling is ________ seconds. (coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu_K=0.25$ and $g=9.8$ m/s$^2$)
question: A solid cylinder having radius $R$ and length $L$ is slipping on a rough horizontal plane. At time $t=0$ the cylinder has a translational velocity $v_0=49$ m/s, perpendicular to its axis and a rotational velocity $v_0/4R$ about the centre. The time taken by the cylinder to start rolling is ________ seconds. (coefficient of kinetic friction $\mu_K=0.25$ and $g=9.8$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Initial translational velocity, $v_0 = 49$ m/s.

Initial angular velocity, $\omega_0 = \dfrac{v_0}{4R}$.

Velocity of the lowest point of the cylinder is $v_{bottom} = v_0 - \omega_0 R = v_0 - \dfrac{v_0}{4} = \dfrac{3v_0}{4} > 0$.

Since the lowest point is slipping forward, kinetic friction $f_k = \mu_k m g$ acts in the backward direction.

Translational acceleration, $a = -\dfrac{f_k}{m} = -\mu_k g$.

Angular acceleration, $\alpha = \dfrac{f_k R}{I} = \dfrac{\mu_k m g R}{\dfrac{1}{2} m R^2} = \dfrac{2\mu_k g}{R}$.

At time $t$, the translational velocity is $v = v_0 - \mu_k g t$.

The angular velocity is $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t = \dfrac{v_0}{4R} + \dfrac{2\mu_k g}{R} t$.

For pure rolling to start, $v = \omega R$.

$v_0 - \mu_k g t = \left( \dfrac{v_0}{4R} + \dfrac{2\mu_k g}{R} t \right) R$

$v_0 - \mu_k g t = \dfrac{v_0}{4} + 2\mu_k g t$

$3\mu_k g t = \dfrac{3v_0}{4}$

$t = \dfrac{v_0}{4\mu_k g}$

Substituting the given values $v_0 = 49$ m/s, $\mu_k = 0.25$, and $g = 9.8$ m/s$^2$:

$t = \dfrac{49}{4 \times 0.25 \times 9.8} = \dfrac{49}{9.8} = 5$ s.

Answer: $5$

subject: Physics
q:
A 34 m long ladder weighing 10 kg leans on a frictionless wall. Its feet rest on the floor 3 m away from the wall as shown in the figure. If Ff and Fw are the reaction forces of the floor and the wall, then ratio of FwFf will be :
(Use g=10 m s-2.)
question:
A 34 m long ladder weighing 10 kg leans on a frictionless wall. Its feet rest on the floor 3 m away from the wall as shown in the figure. If Ff and Fw are the reaction forces of the floor and the wall, then ratio of FwFf will be :
(Use g=10 m s-2.)
solution:
Applying force balance in vertical direction

N1=mg=100 N

Applying torque balance at the bottom contact point,

mg×32=N2×5⇒N2=Fw=30 N

Applying force balance in horizontal direction

fs=N2=30 N

Net reaction at the floor will be,

Ff=N12+fs2=1002+302=10109 N

Therefore, FwFf=3010109=3109

subject: Physics
q: A person of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is sitting on a swing of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and swinging with and an angular amplitude <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the person stands up when the swing passes through its lowest point, the work done by him, assuming that his centre of mass moves by a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>l</mi><mo><</mo><mo><</mo><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> is close to:
question: A person of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is sitting on a swing of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and swinging with and an angular amplitude <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If the person stands up when the swing passes through its lowest point, the work done by him, assuming that his centre of mass moves by a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>l</mi><mo><</mo><mo><</mo><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> is close to:
solution:
From angular momentum conservation
MV0L=MV(L-l)V=V0LL-l
Now
W=Mgl+12MV02LL-l2-V02
Since l≪L
W=Mgl+12MV021-lL-2-V02=Mgl+12MV021+2lL-V02=Mgl+MV02lL
From SHM
V0=ωA=gL×Lθ0=gLθ0
Using this
W=Mgl+MgL×θ02×lLW=Mgl1+θ02

subject: Physics
q: Let the moment of inertia of a hollow cylinder of length <math><mn>30</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> (inner radius <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> and outer radius <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> ), about its axis be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>I</mtext></math> . The radius of a thin cylinder of the same mass such that its moment of inertia about its axis is also <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>I</mtext><mo>,</mo></math> is:
question: Let the moment of inertia of a hollow cylinder of length <math><mn>30</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> (inner radius <math><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> and outer radius <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> ), about its axis be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>I</mtext></math> . The radius of a thin cylinder of the same mass such that its moment of inertia about its axis is also <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>I</mtext><mo>,</mo></math> is:
solution:
Moment of inertia of a hollow cylinder whose inner radius id r and outer radius is R is given as 

I=m(R2+r2)2

m202+1022=mk2

K=400+1002; K=250

K=510 cm

subject: Physics
q: A uniform rod AB is suspended from a point X, at a variable distance x from A, as shown. To make the rod horizontal, a mass m is suspended from its end A. A set of (m, x) value is recorded. The appropriate variables that give a straight line, when plotted, are:
question: A uniform rod AB is suspended from a point X, at a variable distance x from A, as shown. To make the rod horizontal, a mass m is suspended from its end A. A set of (m, x) value is recorded. The appropriate variables that give a straight line, when plotted, are:
solution:
Net torque about suspension is zero, 
⇒mgx=Mgl2-x⇒mx=Ml2-Mx
⇒m=Ml21x-M⇒y= α1x-C, where,

y=m, a=Ml2 and C=M
it is the equation of straight line with variable m and 1x.

subject: Physics
q: Two discs of same mass and different radii are made of different materials such that their thicknesses are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The densities of materials are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The moment of inertia of these discs respectively about their diameters will be in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: Two discs of same mass and different radii are made of different materials such that their thicknesses are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The densities of materials are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. The moment of inertia of these discs respectively about their diameters will be in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Mass of the disc in terms of density can be written as, m=ρπR2t.

⇒R2=mρπt

Now moment of inertia of the disc about the diameter is, mR24.

Therefore,

I=mR24=m24ρπt⇒I∝1ρt

Required ratio, I1I2=ρ2t2ρ1t1=53×0.51=56

Hence, x=5.

subject: Physics
q: Solid sphere A is rotating about an axis PQ. If the radius of the sphere is 5 cm, then its radius of gyration about PQ will be x cm. The value of x is _____.
question: Solid sphere A is rotating about an axis PQ. If the radius of the sphere is 5 cm, then its radius of gyration about PQ will be x cm. The value of x is _____.
solution:
Using parallel axis theorem,

IPQ=ICOM +Md2

=25MR2+Md2

=M25×52+102

=M[110]=Mk2

⇒k=110 cm

Therefore, x=110.

subject: Physics
q: A thin circular ring of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is rotating with a constant angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal plane about an axis vertical to its plane and passing through the center of the ring. If two objects each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> be attached gently to the opposite ends of a diameter of ring, the ring will then rotate with an angular velocity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
question: A thin circular ring of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is rotating with a constant angular velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal plane about an axis vertical to its plane and passing through the center of the ring. If two objects each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> be attached gently to the opposite ends of a diameter of ring, the ring will then rotate with an angular velocity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>).
solution:
As the masses are gently placed we can apply conservation of angular momentum.

Initial moment of Inertia I1=MR2

Final moment of inertia I2=MR2+2mR2

By conservation of angular momentum

I1ω1=I2ω2⇒MR2×2=M+2mR2×ω2

⇒ω2=2MM+2m rad s-1

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical vessel containing a liquid is rotated about its axis so that the liquid rises at its sides as shown in the figure. The radius of vessel is 5 cm and the angular speed of rotation is ω rad s-1. The difference in the height, h in cm of liquid at the Centre of vessel and at the sides of the vessel will be :
question: A cylindrical vessel containing a liquid is rotated about its axis so that the liquid rises at its sides as shown in the figure. The radius of vessel is 5 cm and the angular speed of rotation is ω rad s-1. The difference in the height, h in cm of liquid at the Centre of vessel and at the sides of the vessel will be :
solution:
ρdrω2r=ρgdh

ω2∫0Rr dr=g∫0hdh

ω2R22=gh

 h=ω2R22g=25ω22g cm.

subject: Physics
q: A circular disc $X$ of radius $R$ is made from an iron plate of thickness $t$, and another disc $Y$ of radius $4 \mathrm{R}$ is made from an iron plate of thickness $\frac{\mathrm{t}}{4}$. Then the relation between the moment of inertia $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{X}}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{Y}}$ is
question: A circular disc $X$ of radius $R$ is made from an iron plate of thickness $t$, and another disc $Y$ of radius $4 \mathrm{R}$ is made from an iron plate of thickness $\frac{\mathrm{t}}{4}$. Then the relation between the moment of inertia $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{X}}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{Y}}$ is
solution:
$$
\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mR} \quad \text { or } \quad \mathrm{M} \propto \mathrm{t} \propto \mathrm{R}^2
$$
For $\operatorname{disc} X, I_x=\frac{1}{2}(m)(R)^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\pi r^2 t\right) .(R)^2$
For disc $Y, I_y=\frac{1}{2}\left[\pi(4 R)^2 \cdot t / 4\right][4 R]^2$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{I_x}{I_y}=\frac{1}{(4)^3} \Rightarrow I_y=64 I_x
$$

subject: Physics
q: A circular ring and a solid sphere having same radius roll down on an inclined plane from rest without slipping. The ratio of their velocities when reached at the bottom of the plane is $\sqrt{\frac{x}{5}}$ where $\mathrm{x}=$ _____
question: A circular ring and a solid sphere having same radius roll down on an inclined plane from rest without slipping. The ratio of their velocities when reached at the bottom of the plane is $\sqrt{\frac{x}{5}}$ where $\mathrm{x}=$ _____
solution:
Applying Mechanical Energy conservation :
$\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{f}}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 0+\mathrm{Mgh}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{k}^2}{\mathrm{R}^2}\right)+0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~V}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{gh}}{1+\frac{\mathrm{k}^2}{\mathrm{R}^2}}}
\end{aligned}$
So Ratio of velocities
$\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {Ring }}}{\mathrm{V}_{\text {solids sphere }}}=\sqrt{\frac{1+\frac{2}{5}}{1+1}}=\sqrt{\frac{7}{10}}$
$\mathrm{x}=3.5$ Rounding off $\mathrm{x}=4$

subject: Physics
q: The moment of inertia of a semicircular ring about an axis, passing through the center and perpendicular to the plane of ring, is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><msup><mi>MR</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the radius and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is the mass of the semicircular ring. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></math>.
question: The moment of inertia of a semicircular ring about an axis, passing through the center and perpendicular to the plane of ring, is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><msup><mi>MR</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the radius and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is the mass of the semicircular ring. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi></math>.
solution:
The moment of inertia of a semicircular ring about a line perpendicular to the plane of the ring and passing through its centre is given as I=MR2, where m and R are the mass and radius of the ring. Comparing the given value

.I= MR2=1xMR2

⇒x=1

subject: Physics
q: A thin circular plate of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> has its density varying as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">ρ</mtext><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><msub><mtext>=ρ</mtext><mtext>0</mtext></msub><mtext mathvariant="italic">r</mtext></math> with <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> as constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the distance from its centre. The moment of Inertia of the circular plate about an axis perpendicular to the plate and passing through its edge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of the coefficient <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is:
question: A thin circular plate of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> has its density varying as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext mathvariant="italic">ρ</mtext><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><msub><mtext>=ρ</mtext><mtext>0</mtext></msub><mtext mathvariant="italic">r</mtext></math> with <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> as constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the distance from its centre. The moment of Inertia of the circular plate about an axis perpendicular to the plate and passing through its edge is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of the coefficient <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is:
solution:
dm=2πrdr×ρ=2πρ0r2dr
M=2πρ0∫0Rr2dr=23πρ0R3
ICM=ρdmr2=2πρ0∫r4dr=25πρ0R5
Icm=35MR2
I=Icm+MR2=85MR2

subject: Physics
q: For a particle in uniform circular motion the acceleration $\vec{a}$ at a point $P(R, \theta)$ on the circle of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is (here $\theta$ is measured from the $x$-axis)
question: For a particle in uniform circular motion the acceleration $\vec{a}$ at a point $P(R, \theta)$ on the circle of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is (here $\theta$ is measured from the $x$-axis)
solution: For a particle in uniform circular motion, $$ \begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}} \text { towards centre of circle } \\ & \therefore \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}}(-\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \\ & \text { or } \quad \vec{a}=-\frac{v^2}{R} \cos \theta \hat{i}-\frac{v^2}{R} \sin \theta \hat{j} \\ & \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum distances of a comet from the Sun are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively. If the speed of the comet at the nearest point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the speed at the farthest point is
question: The maximum and minimum distances of a comet from the Sun are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> respectively. If the speed of the comet at the nearest point is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the speed at the farthest point is
solution:
By angular momentum conservation :

mv1r1=mv2r2

v1=48×10141.6×1012=3000 m s-1

=3×103 m s-1.

subject: Physics
q: In a physical balance working on the principle of moments, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mg</mi></math> weight is placed on the left pan, the beam becomes horizontal. Both the empty pans of the balance are of equal mass. Which of the following statements is correct?
question: In a physical balance working on the principle of moments, when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mg</mi></math> weight is placed on the left pan, the beam becomes horizontal. Both the empty pans of the balance are of equal mass. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: Every object that is weighed using this balance appears lighter than its actual weight
Left arm is shorter than the right arm
Both the arms are of same length
Left arm is longer than the right arm
solution:
Let m 1 and m 2 be the masses of pan

It is given that m1=m2



Since balance is achieved, so net torque about O should be zero.
5 mg+ m 1 g r 1 = m 2 g r 2
⇒5mg+m1gr1=m1gr2
⇒r1r2=m1gm1g+5mg
⇒r1r2<1

So, left arm is shorter than the right arm.

(Assuming the acceleration to be uniform).
question:

(Assuming the acceleration to be uniform).
solution:
We know, θ=ω1+ω22t

Let number of revolutions be N

∴2πN=2π900+246060×2×26

N=728

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of radius $4$ cm and mass $5$ kg is rotating (rotation axis is passing through the centre of the sphere) with an angular velocity of $1200$ rpm. It is brought to rest in $10$ s by applying a constant torque. The torque applied and the number of rotations it made before it comes to rest are _______ and _______ respectively.
question: A solid sphere of radius $4$ cm and mass $5$ kg is rotating (rotation axis is passing through the centre of the sphere) with an angular velocity of $1200$ rpm. It is brought to rest in $10$ s by applying a constant torque. The torque applied and the number of rotations it made before it comes to rest are _______ and _______ respectively.
solution:
Initial angular velocity $\omega_0 = 1200 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{1200 \times 2\pi}{60} = 40\pi \text{ rad/s}$

Final angular velocity $\omega = 0$

Time $t = 10 \text{ s}$

Angular acceleration $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega - \omega_0}{t} = \dfrac{0 - 40\pi}{10} = -4\pi \text{ rad/s}^2$

Moment of inertia of the solid sphere $I = \dfrac{2}{5}MR^2 = \dfrac{2}{5} \times 5 \times (0.04)^2 = 0.0032 \text{ kg m}^2$

Torque $\tau = I|\alpha| = 0.0032 \times 4\pi = 0.0128\pi \text{ Nm}$

Angle rotated $\theta = \dfrac{\omega_0 + \omega}{2}t = \dfrac{40\pi + 0}{2} \times 10 = 200\pi \text{ rad}$

Number of rotations $N = \dfrac{\theta}{2\pi} = \dfrac{200\pi}{2\pi} = 100$

Answer: $0.0128\pi$ Nm, $100$

subject: Physics
q: Moment of inertia of a disc of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>' about any of its diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. The moment of inertia of this disc about an axis normal to the disc and passing through a point on its edge will be, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: Moment of inertia of a disc of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>' about any of its diameter is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math>. The moment of inertia of this disc about an axis normal to the disc and passing through a point on its edge will be, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>M</mi><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Given here, 

Moment of inertia of a disc of mass M and radius 'R' about any of its diameter is MR24. 

Moment of inertia of disc about centre is Icm=MR24+MR24=MR22

Using parallel axis theorem, we can write

I=Icm+Md2

=MR22+MR2

=32MR2

Hence, the value of x=3.

subject: Physics
q: A solid cylinder is released from rest from the top of an inclined plane of inclination 30° and length 60 cm. If the cylinder rolls without slipping, its speed upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane is ______ m s-1. (Given g=10 m s-2)
question: A solid cylinder is released from rest from the top of an inclined plane of inclination 30° and length 60 cm. If the cylinder rolls without slipping, its speed upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane is ______ m s-1. (Given g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Let speed of cylinder upon reaching the bottom of the inclined plane be v.

Here, gain in kinetic energy=loss in potential energy

So, 12Iω2+12mv2=mglsinθ

⇒12mr22ω2+12mv2=mglsin30°

For pure rolling, v=rω

⇒12mr22vr2+12mv2=mglsin30°

⇒14mv2+12mv2=mglsin30°⇒34mv2=mgl2⇒v=4gl6

Putting the values, we have

⇒v=4×10×0.66=2 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: One end of a straight uniform 1m long bar is pivoted on horizontal table. It is released from rest when it makes an angle 30o from the horizontal (see figure). Its angular speed when it hits the table is given as n rad s-1 , where n is an integer. The value of n is ____________
question: One end of a straight uniform 1m long bar is pivoted on horizontal table. It is released from rest when it makes an angle 30o from the horizontal (see figure). Its angular speed when it hits the table is given as n rad s-1 , where n is an integer. The value of n is ____________
solution:
according to energy conservation

mgl2sin⁡30°=12ml23ω2
Solving
ω2=15
ω=15

subject: Physics
q:
Match List-I with List-II

  List-I   List-II
(A) Moment of inertia of solid sphere
of radius R about any tangent.		 (I) 53MR2
(B) Moment of inertia of hollow sphere of radius
R about any tangent.		 (II) 75MR2
(C) Moment of inertia of circular ring of radius
R about its diameter.		 (III) 14MR2
(D) Moment of inertia of circular disc of radius
R about any diameter.		 (IV) 12MR2
question:
Match List-I with List-II

  List-I   List-II
(A) Moment of inertia of solid sphere
of radius R about any tangent.		 (I) 53MR2
(B) Moment of inertia of hollow sphere of radius
R about any tangent.		 (II) 75MR2
(C) Moment of inertia of circular ring of radius
R about its diameter.		 (III) 14MR2
(D) Moment of inertia of circular disc of radius
R about any diameter.		 (IV) 12MR2
solution:
For solid sphere

I0=Icom +MR2  (Using parallel axis theorem)

I0=25MR2+MR2

I0=75MR2⇒A→II

For hollow sphere

I=Icom+MR2⇒I=53MR2⇒B→I

For Circular ring

I1+I2=I3 ( Using perpendicular axis theorem)

By symmetry MOI

About 1 and 2 Axis are same i.e.

I1=I2

∴  2I1=I3=MR2   ∵Icom=MR2

I1=MR22⇒ C→IV

Similarly in disc

2I1=MR22∵Icom=MR22

I1=MR24⇒D→III

q: Two discs have moments of intertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> about their respective axes perpendicular to the plane and passing through the centre. They are rotating with angular speeds, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively and are brought into contact face to face with their axes of rotation coaxial. The loss in kinetic energy of the system in the process is given by:
question: Two discs have moments of intertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> about their respective axes perpendicular to the plane and passing through the centre. They are rotating with angular speeds, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively and are brought into contact face to face with their axes of rotation coaxial. The loss in kinetic energy of the system in the process is given by:
solution:
Angular momentum conservation

I1ω1+I2ω2=I1+I2ω

ω=I1ω1+I2ω2I1+I2

Loss =12I1ω12+12I2ω22-12I1+I2ω2

=12I1ω12+12I2ω22-12I1+I2I1ω1+I2ω2I1+I22

=12I1I2I1+I2ω1-ω22

Ei-Ef=I1I2ω1-ω222I1+I2

solution:
I=2×25ma2+2×25ma2+mb2

I=85ma2+2mb2

subject: Physics
q: Two iron solid discs of negligible thickness have radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ and moment of intertia $I_1$ and $I_2$, respectively. For $R_2=2 R_1$, the ratio of $I_1$ and $I_2$ would be $1 / x$, where $\mathrm{x}=$ ________ -
question: Two iron solid discs of negligible thickness have radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ and moment of intertia $I_1$ and $I_2$, respectively. For $R_2=2 R_1$, the ratio of $I_1$ and $I_2$ would be $1 / x$, where $\mathrm{x}=$ ________ -
solution: Given $R_2=2 R_1$ $\begin{aligned} & M_1=\sigma \times \pi R_1^2=M_o \\ & M_2=\sigma \times \pi R_2^2=M_o \\ & M_2=\sigma \times \pi R_2^2=\sigma \times \pi\left[2 R_1\right]^2=\sigma \times 4 \pi R_1^2=4 M_o \end{aligned}$ $\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{\frac{M_1 R_1^2}{2}}{\frac{M_2 R_2^2}{2}}=\frac{M_1 R_1^2}{M_2 R_2^2}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{16}$

subject: Physics
q: From a solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, a cube of the maximum possible volume is cut. Moment of inertia of cube about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to one of its faces is:
question: From a solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, a cube of the maximum possible volume is cut. Moment of inertia of cube about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to one of its faces is:
solution:
Let a be the length of edge for the cube, with maximum possible volume diagonal length =2R⇒3a=2R⇒a=2R3. As densities of sphere and cube are equal. Let M' be mass of the cube, M43πR3=M'a3⇒M′=3Ma34πR3. Moment of inertia of cube about an axis passing through its center is,  I=M′2a212 =3Ma34πR3×2a212 =Ma58πR3.

also, a=23R⇒I =M×32 R58π×93R3 =4MR293π.

subject: Physics
q: A fly wheel is accelerated uniformly from rest and rotates through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>rad</mi></math> in the first second. The angle rotated by the fly wheel in the next second, will be :
question: A fly wheel is accelerated uniformly from rest and rotates through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>rad</mi></math> in the first second. The angle rotated by the fly wheel in the next second, will be :
solution:
Here the angular acceleration is constant, so we have,

θ=ωt+12αt2.

When flywheel is rotated from rest in the first second, then θ1=12α12=5 rad 

⇒α=10 rad s-2

The angle turned by flywheel in the next second is θ2=12α22=12104=20 rad s-2

Thus, the angle turned during next second is θ=20-5=15 rad

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere ($A$) of mass $5m$ and a spherical shell ($B$) of mass $m$, both having same radius, are placed on a rough surface. When a force of same magnitude is applied tangentially at the highest points of $A$ and $B$, they start rolling without slipping with an acceleration of $a_A$ and $a_B$, respectively. The ratio of $a_A$ and $a_B$ is __________.
question: A solid sphere ($A$) of mass $5m$ and a spherical shell ($B$) of mass $m$, both having same radius, are placed on a rough surface. When a force of same magnitude is applied tangentially at the highest points of $A$ and $B$, they start rolling without slipping with an acceleration of $a_A$ and $a_B$, respectively. The ratio of $a_A$ and $a_B$ is __________.
solution:
For a body of mass $M$ and radius $R$ rolling without slipping under a force $F$ applied tangentially at the highest point, the torque about the point of contact with the ground is $\tau = F(2R)$.

The moment of inertia about the point of contact is $I = I_{cm} + MR^2$.

Using $\tau = I\alpha$ and the rolling without slipping condition $a = \alpha R$, we get:

$F(2R) = (I_{cm} + MR^2) \dfrac{a}{R} \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{2FR^2}{I_{cm} + MR^2}$

For the solid sphere $A$ of mass $5m$:

$I_{cm,A} = \dfrac{2}{5}(5m)R^2 = 2mR^2$

$a_A = \dfrac{2FR^2}{2mR^2 + 5mR^2} = \dfrac{2F}{7m}$

For the spherical shell $B$ of mass $m$:

$I_{cm,B} = \dfrac{2}{3}mR^2$

$a_B = \dfrac{2FR^2}{\dfrac{2}{3}mR^2 + mR^2} = \dfrac{2F}{\dfrac{5}{3}m} = \dfrac{6F}{5m}$

The ratio of their accelerations is:

$\dfrac{a_A}{a_B} = \dfrac{\dfrac{2F}{7m}}{\dfrac{6F}{5m}} = \dfrac{2}{7} \times \dfrac{5}{6} = \dfrac{5}{21}$

Answer: $5 : 21$

subject: Physics
q: A solid disc of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is rotating with an angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math>, about an axis normal to its circular plane and passing through its centre of mass. The retarding torque required to bring the disc at rest in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is _________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>
question: A solid disc of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is rotating with an angular velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rpm</mi></math>, about an axis normal to its circular plane and passing through its centre of mass. The retarding torque required to bring the disc at rest in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is _________<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>
solution:
τ=ΔLΔt=Iωf-ωiΔt

τ=mR22×[0-ω]Δt

=10×20×10-222×600×π30×10

=0.4π=4π×10-1

The moment of intertia of the system about an axis through the centroid and perpendicular to the plane of triangle, will be _______ $\mathrm{kg} \mathrm{m}^2$.
solution: Moment of inertia about $\mathrm{C}$ and perpendicular to the plane is : $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} & =\mathrm{r}^2[2+4+6] \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \times 12 \\ \mathrm{I} & =4 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2 \end{aligned}$

q: A circular disc of radius b has a hole of radius a at its centre(see figure). If the mass per unit area of the disc varies as σ0r then, the radius of gyration of the disc about its axis passing through the center is
question: A circular disc of radius b has a hole of radius a at its centre(see figure). If the mass per unit area of the disc varies as σ0r then, the radius of gyration of the disc about its axis passing through the center is

=σ0r2 πrdr
m=2πσ0∫abdr=2π σ0(b-a)

Radius of gyration =Im =23π σ0b3-a32π σ0b-a

=b2+a2+ab3.
